data.title: When 5 million AI agents flooded Telegram
data.description:
Author: Sleepy.md, Kenny (TON Foundation)

In the first few months of 2026, those caught in the AI ​​wave were almost entirely drawn to two steep curves.

One ​​curve concerns the fervor surrounding open source. OpenClaw garnered over 350,000 stars on GitHub at an unprecedented rate, breaking the record for open source project growth. The mainstream success of "Little Lobster" is undeniable.

The other curve concerns the disruption of business. Anthropic, leveraging Claude's strong performance in code scenarios, has sharply torn open a market gap.

Its annual recurring revenue (ARR) surged past the $30 billion mark, leaving the former king, OpenAI, behind and quietly reshaping the landscape of the gaming world. For some time now, these two curves have dominated the pages of almost every tech media outlet. People are hotly debating the limits of large-scale models, discussing the commercialization of Coding Agents, and also harboring concerns about how much of the inflated valuations are just the tip of the iceberg. However, beyond the clamor of the spotlight, a profound and silent transformation is taking place on another platform. In early 2026, the number of active bots on Telegram surged from approximately 3 million to over 8 million in just a few weeks. It's important to understand that the initial 3 million was a foundation Telegram had slowly built up over nearly a decade since launching its Bot ecosystem in 2015. Now, in just a few weeks, that number has doubled, with a remarkably steep growth curve. These newly added Bots are almost all user-created OpenClaw AI Agents. Meanwhile, in the official OpenClaw documentation, Telegram became the first instant messaging platform to be fully explained, with the most complete configuration tutorials and the most active community discussions. Interestingly, this wave presents a divide in the Chinese-speaking world. Locals only knew of Telegram by name, but didn't actually use it; while overseas users, AI practitioners, and those more inclined to try things out themselves naturally chose Telegram as the platform for deploying agents. While everyone is eagerly chasing the agent trend, not everyone has realized that Telegram is the most suitable habitat for these new digital species to take root. Why Telegram? To answer this question, we might first need to look back at a chapter in the history of the internet. In Geneva in 1991, Tim Berners-Lee typed code on a NeXT computer, building the first website in human history using the HTTP protocol. It was a golden age of pure openness, belonging to pioneers. On this invisible network, anyone could freely build a website, and countless endpoints were interconnected. There were no arrogant gatekeepers, nor were there any checkpoints or toll booths. The design philosophy of the TCP/IP protocol, ingrained in its very core, is to distribute power equally and without reservation to every node. But thirty years later, this grand narrative of freedom ended with the App Store. Apple takes 30% of every transaction, and so does Google. Developers must undergo review, comply with platform content rules, and be prepared for app removal before submitting an app. This may be an inevitable consequence of commercial evolution; the closed system has indeed honed a more refined user experience. However, at the same time, the scepter of power has irrevocably been consolidated in the hands of a very few giants. This consolidation is essentially a legacy of the graphical user interface (GUI) era; giants control every pixel on the screen, thus controlling distribution rights. But the emergence of Agents is fundamentally dismantling this pixel hegemony. Agents pursue "intent recognition," requiring no cumbersome buttons or navigation, only a dialogue. This means that the super gateway to the AI ​​era will shift from cold desktop icons to warm, context-rich social chat windows. Thus, IM (Instant Messaging) became the most solid foundation supporting Agents. Some might argue that even without IM, communicating with Agents directly through Claude terminals or other means would be more efficient. However, this is ultimately the choice of a minority of the global population. From the perspective of Agents' mass adoption, IM still occupies the broadest and best ecological niche. However, if this habitat remains closed, it's merely another digital land grab. This is precisely why Telegram seems irreplaceable. Compared to other IM platforms that attempt to confine Agents to private traffic, Telegram has taken a completely different path. It provides more than just a dialogue window; it inherits Tim Berners-Lee's original dream: decentralization, open protocols, and a natural wariness of gatekeepers. The journey of founder Pavel Durov in creating Telegram is fundamentally a story of growth filled with struggle. In his early years, he single-handedly built VK, the largest social network in his homeland, but resolutely refused to hand over users' private information when faced with strong pressure from the authorities, leading him to leave the country. He and his brother Nikolai led a small team to Dubai, where they established Telegram with their own money. In 2018, when Russian authorities demanded that Telegram hand over its encryption keys, Durov again firmly refused. This person has etched his product philosophy into Telegram's DNA, giving it permissionless bot creation, an open and comprehensive API, and a strong commitment to privacy. Now, he's extending this freedom to Agents. Faced with the influx of Agents, Telegram has never been a passive recipient. It is consciously expanding and reshaping itself from a top-three global social network with over 1 billion active users into an infrastructure for the symbiotic interaction between humans and Agents. In the foreseeable future, the interaction mode between humans and AI is gradually converging into a sophisticated three-layer structure: command-line interface (CLI), agent, and protocol. The CLI (Channel Interface) is responsible for receiving user intents, allowing users to directly express their needs using natural language instead of manipulating complex graphical interfaces. The Agent is responsible for understanding intents, breaking down tasks, and executing them. The Protocol further connects services, funds, and permissions, enabling the Agent to do more than just "chat"; it truly possesses the ability to interact with the external world and perform trusted executions. The unique aspect of Telegram's ecosystem lies in its gradual integration of these three layers into a complete system.

However, within this three-layer structure, one detail is particularly worth highlighting: Telegram has recently launched several features, and now Bots can create and manage other Bots, and Bots can also interact with each other. This is more like a signal of species evolution. All past Agents were essentially "single-celled organisms," only able to respond to human commands and complete a single task assigned by humans. When Agents begin to incubate, direct, and coordinate with other Agents, we witness the birth of a "multicellular organism"—a vibrant, self-sustaining agent collaboration network that no longer requires constant human intervention. This is Telegram's true strategic vision: to teach AI to organize itself. However, a sophisticated framework alone is ultimately insufficient. When 5 million eager agents are poised to work, who will provide them with independent and cost-effective computing power? Who is supplying the agents with the necessary resources? In the preceding framework, the CLI receives the intent, and the Agent is responsible for understanding and decision-making. But a more fundamental question remains: who ultimately performs the computation and execution of these tasks? Most Telegram Agents today don't actually possess independent computing power. Developers can complete dialogues and tool calls by integrating readily available large-scale model APIs. This is currently the easiest and most mainstream shortcut. However, this approach has a problem: the computational execution capability is outsourced. The Agent itself doesn't truly possess a computational execution layer but relies on third-party model services or the local environment to complete tasks. Once the scale increases, issues related to cost, independence, scheduling, and dependencies will gradually emerge. In many AI developer forums, there are daily lamentations of API access being abruptly cut off due to "alleged violations" (such as Anthropic's newly launched KYC verification), and frustration with the high costs of inference. Builders trying to make their agents truly run on Telegram have discovered that entrusting their "brains" to giants is tantamount to handing their necks to others. Therefore, Telegram launched Cocoon to provide a solid "body" for its agents. Cocoon, short for Confidential Compute Open Network, is a decentralized AI inference computing power scheduling and trading network. Computing power is provided by globally distributed GPU node operators, agent developers and users consume this computing power, and payment settlements are completed through the TON blockchain. What it does can be simply understood as a Didi Chuxing-style service that rents out inference computing power. Developers submit requests in the cloud, and this invisible network quickly senses them, scheduling the most suitable nodes to perform inference and computation, and then returning the results. The true ambition of this process is not simply to supply computing power; it acts like an invisible hand, kneading and abstracting those machines and services that were originally scattered across the globe, fighting their own battles, transforming them into something that can be easily deployed—like a computing power version of OpenRouter with an intelligent scheduling hub and on-chain payments. In this process, execution is not just about "completing a task"; each execution can be recorded, measured, and settled through the TON blockchain. In this way, execution is not only schedulable but also has the foundation for being tradable and sustainably operating. This is like the eve of the Second Industrial Revolution, when factories shrouded in smoke were forced to grit their teeth and keep going with their cumbersome steam engines, exhausting themselves in the repetitive cycle of reinventing the wheel. Until the national power grid was built, factory managers only needed to connect a single cable to focus all their energy on production, no longer needing to worry day and night about energy shortages. Cocoon's significance to Agents is similar. This demand is not unfounded. Cocoon launched its first version in November 2025, and Telegram founder Pavel Durov also endorsed this GPU inference computing power network at the conference. Furthermore, the AI ​​Summary (one-click summary of WeChat official account articles) and AI Editor (one-click translation or style and grammar modification of text to be sent) features that the Telegram App has begun to launch utilize computing power from Cocoon. Telegram itself was Cocoon's first major customer during its initial launch. With independent, abundant, reasonably priced, secure, and privacy-preserving computing power, the Agent can finally roll up its sleeves and get to work. But another, more practical problem arises: how can it cleanly pocket the money after putting in the effort? And how can it prove to people that this self-proclaimed capable Agent isn't a fraud? From "Instrument" to "Citizen" On December 31, 1600, Queen Elizabeth I of England signed a Royal Charter, granting a monopoly to the "London Merchants' Company" in the East India Trade. This company later became the East India Company. The historical significance of this event goes far beyond the birth of a trading company. It marked the first time that humanity solemnly bestowed "legal personhood" upon a non-human entity; the company could own property, enter into contracts, and sue and be sued. Before this, all economic activities had to be conducted by natural persons. The emergence of the East India Company signified the first time human society acknowledged that an abstract entity comprised of rules and contracts could exist as an independent economic participant. Four hundred years later, we are experiencing a similar moment. However, this time, it is not a trading company that acquires "legal personhood," but the Agent. An Agent that cannot participate in economic settlement will forever remain merely a tool. TON completes the puzzle of Agents becoming independent economic entities. Verifying identity, invoking services, and completing payments and settlements are not new concepts in the internet world. In an ecosystem like Telegram, where Agent Bots are rapidly growing, these needs are not abstract. As developers begin building products around Bots, Agents, and automated processes, they increasingly require an infrastructure capable of verifying identity, invoking capabilities, and settling payments. An agent must first understand who it is and whether it has truly obtained permission to act on behalf of its owner. In the TON system, this is initially handled by the TON Wallet built into Telegram. Users complete identity verification and authorization through the TON Wallet, allowing the agent to perform operations with clearly defined permissions, rather than remaining at the conversational level. However, in more complex systems, simply possessing a blockchain address is far from sufficient. The system also needs to verify whether this identity is trustworthy. This is precisely what identity protocols like IdentityHub fill in. It transforms developers' code contributions, on-chain behavior, and community participation into verifiable reputation, making "identity" no longer just a string of code, but a role with history and credibility. Furthermore, the Agent's mission should not stop at simply replying to messages; they also need to reach outwards and mobilize external services to run more complex task flows. TON MCP plays a crucial connecting role here, providing a unified set of capability interfaces that allow Agents to combine and collaborate across different services. When Agents begin executing actual tasks, transactions and fees are inevitable. In TON, this layer is handled through the TON Pay SDK and Telegram's built-in TON Wallet system, ensuring that each execution can not only be scheduled but also measured and settled. The Toncoin earned by the Agent can be used in Cocoon to purchase computing power, much like the US dollar is pegged to oil transactions. These three layers of capabilities, when combined, form a more fundamental structure. At this point, the Agent is no longer merely a tool that mechanically calls APIs, but has transformed into a real participant with authorization, its own reputation, and the ability to autonomously allocate services and complete transactions. If Cocoon endowed the Agent with the ability to act, then TON completes the task by allowing these actions to be woven together, linked together, and ultimately integrated into a vast economic cooperation network. This means that Agents have finally stepped out of the cold, rigid human-machine dialogue box and begun to act as real economic actors, becoming involved in the daily torrent of commerce. When the free environment of Telegram, the computing power grid of Cocoon, and the financial lifeline of TON finally converge, what we see is no longer just a super app, but a brand-new digital economy where humans and AI coexist. This is a replay of the East India Company moment in 1600, where non-human "legal entities" gained the economic power to change the course of the world. Now that the framework of the New World has been laid, where are those pioneers who first held the tickets? Let's return to the chasm mentioned at the beginning. The difference between the two sides of that chasm isn't between "understanding agents" and "not understanding agents," but rather between "taking action on Telegram" and "observing." The 8 million bots on Telegram aren't part of any company's product plan, but rather the natural choice of countless developers. Telegram and the TON ecosystem, based on over a billion users and millions of agent bots worldwide, are building the best entry point for a collaborative economy between humans and AI. The more important question now is how to actually make it happen. As a highlight of the fourth day of the 2026 Hong Kong Web3 Carnival, the TON Foundation will present a full-day event on April 23rd at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre (Stage 1). The old world's nautical charts are destined not to trace the path to the New World. With 5 million Agents already quietly assembling on-chain wallets and computing power in the world of Telegram, what are you waiting for?

data.title: The "security promises" of the Silicon Valley grand model are making the world feel insecure.
data.description:
Author: Li Hailun, Tencent Technology

Recently, Anthropic and OpenAI have been pushing for one thing:Changing the way traditional security vulnerability discovery and analysis capabilities are produced. On April 7th, Anthropic released a preview version of its next-generation model, Claude Mythos, and launched "Project Glasswing," triggering a chain reaction in the market. Even before its public release, Mythos caused immense market panic. Because as soon as someone discovered a vulnerability, someone else exploited it, even turning it into offensive capabilities. Security issues have never been so severe. On the first trading day after Mythos's release, the stock prices of several security vendors, including CrowdStrike, Palo Alto Networks, and Zscaler, experienced significant declines, with some falling by double digits. OpenAI may have understood people's expectation that "the devil is one foot tall, but the saint is ten feet tall." On April 15th, OpenAI launched GPT-5.4-Cyber: a "privileged model" for cybersecurity defenders. OpenAI also announced the expansion of its Trusted Access for Cybersecurity (TAC) program, opening it to thousands of verified individual defenders and hundreds of teams responsible for critical software security. While seemingly a competition between two large modeling companies, these events represent a new attempt by major US modeling companies to enhance their security capabilities. An engineer with many years of experience in cybersecurity frankly admitted that his first reaction to this news was a sense of crisis: "The key to these two events isn't how powerful the models are, but rather that the way we find vulnerabilities has changed in the future." Li Guanghui, founder and CEO of AI security company BraneMatrix, stated, "Traditional cybersecurity issues are being restructured algorithmically, and the security of the algorithms themselves will rise to the level of AI-native security, becoming a core technological capability in the new era." Why does the release of such large-scale models bring about such a significant spillover effect? ​​If vulnerability discovery capabilities can be replicated at scale using models, is the capability threshold for the future cybersecurity industry being rewritten? How will the competitive landscape of the industry change? Change is coming. Traditional cybersecurity relies primarily on human experience, judgment, and long-term accumulation. A senior cybersecurity engineer said, "Some people discover vulnerabilities, some exploit them, and some patch them; attack methods are constantly evolving, and defense methods follow suit. Therefore, in the past, no one in the security industry possessed complete security capabilities from the outset, and no company could maintain a leading position indefinitely; everything relied on accumulation." In the cybersecurity industry, there is a time lag between attack and defense. Vulnerabilities are discovered, exploited, and then patched; new tools constantly emerge, and capabilities gradually spread through practice. Whether it's an internal enterprise team or a security vendor like CrowdStrike or Palo Alto Networks, they are essentially doing the same thing: productizing complex security capabilities and then delivering them to enterprise customers. However, with the entry of large-scale AI model vendors, a different path has emerged. Security capabilities are no longer reliant on people and experience, or on long-term combat accumulation; instead, some capabilities are being directly encapsulated into models, becoming readily available and callable. Whether it's Mythos or GPT-5.4-Cyber, we need to be further vigilant about the allocation logic of these new security capabilities and their potential impact. A key highlight of GPT-5.4-Cyber ​​is its fine-tuning for defensive security use cases, reducing the rejection rate for legitimate security operations. Previously, when engineers asked AI to help test system vulnerabilities or analyze suspicious code, it might refuse outright because it couldn't distinguish between defense and attack. GPT-5.4-Cyber ​​specifically addresses this issue, "lowering the barrier" for security researchers. Furthermore, its deployment employs a tiered trust mechanism, which is more open than Mythos's "list-based" approach. While Anthropic's Mythos isn't specifically designed for cybersecurity training, its general-purpose capabilities have overflowed into the security field. It can understand code, discover vulnerabilities, and even reproduce and exploit them on its own, making it significantly more powerful than previous models. According to official disclosures, Mythos has discovered thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities in mainstream operating systems and browsers, many of which are high-risk. In some capabilities, it even surpasses most security engineers, and the entire process requires virtually no human intervention, completing it autonomously. Compared to the improvement in capabilities themselves, what deserves our more attention is this: Regardless of the model type, when the code understanding and security analysis capabilities of a large model are strong enough, once restrictions are lifted or authorization is granted, it can be used to discover vulnerabilities and even be transformed into attack capabilities. Furthermore, when a large model possesses this capability, humans are no longer the sole source of capability. Replacement and Impact Many practitioners in discussions have reached a consensus—in the short term, such replacement will not occur. "People tend to oversimplify the issue," said Li Guanghui. "Security capabilities are not a single technical capability, but a complete system." The traditional cybersecurity industry spans multiple sub-sectors, including network protection, identity authentication, threat intelligence, and risk control systems. These capabilities cannot be covered solely by model inference. Li Guanghui believes that the traditional security industry covers numerous sub-sectors such as network security, identity authentication, threat intelligence, and risk control, among which basic enterprise security services still have strong demand. "Moreover, the accumulation of threat intelligence data by traditional large companies, such as non-public IOC intelligence databases including IPs involved in black and gray market attacks and file hash values ​​(converting file content into fixed-length strings through specific algorithms), remains a barrier that models like Mythos cannot replace in the short term. In scenarios requiring specialized data support, such as black and gray market attack detection and internet risk control, the model currently does not show a clear advantage in substitution," he said. Moreover, the "last mile" of security still heavily relies on human intervention. A senior penetration testing engineer illustrated this point: a complete penetration test often requires starting from the exposed internet surface and gradually penetrating the enterprise's intranet and even core systems. This process not only involves dealing with complex system environments but also constantly bypassing non-standardized protection mechanisms such as CAPTCHAs, human-machine verification, and dynamic policies. "Many times, what truly determines success or failure is not the tool itself, but on-site judgment and impromptu strategies." However, while substantial alternatives are unlikely in the short term, the impact is unavoidable. What we need to confront now is that change is already underway. As large-scale models rapidly take over previously highly manual processes such as vulnerability analysis, code auditing, and attack path deduction, operating at lower costs and higher frequencies, the value structure of the security industry is being reshaped: basic capabilities are being compressed, while advanced capabilities are being amplified. "Once this shift occurs, the entire industry's operational logic will be restructured," said a senior security researcher. "With the improvement of AI's coding and understanding capabilities, security capabilities themselves will be greatly amplified, and the traditional cybersecurity system relying on human experience will gradually be weakened. This change is not a replacement, but a shift in the industry's center of gravity." Risks and Crises Anthropic officially stated that Mythos is a "defense-only" model, only available within a closed consortium of 52 technology companies. Members include Amazon, Apple, Broadcom, Cisco, Google, Microsoft, and Nvidia. Anthropic's reasoning is that Mythos' security measures are not yet mature; fully opening it up would mean simultaneously handing attack tools to both defenders and attackers. Therefore, the major model vendor wants to allow partners to use it first, fixing vulnerabilities in critical infrastructure, before gradually opening it up. Its considerations are reasonable, but they have also raised concerns within the industry: because, in the actual context of the security industry, "defense" itself is never a purely theoretical concept. Actions like penetration testing, red team exercises, and vulnerability verification in cybersecurity, which appear to be "attacks," are precisely the foundation of defense. For example, security research has found that large model companies impose many restrictions on their advanced models to prevent them from being used for attacks. However, through unconventional methods (such as using classical Chinese, Sanskrit, or Latin to "circumvent" these restrictions), some large models may still break through these restrictions under certain circumstances, entering a state where their output is almost unconstrained. Therefore, if someone has the ability to bypass these restrictions, they can use these models for attacks, not just defense. In other words, defense, in essence, is "attack simulation for the sake of defense." When the model's code understanding and security analysis capabilities are strong enough, the boundary between offense and defense quickly becomes blurred, making it difficult for vendors to truly and precisely control this boundary. Besides security considerations, there are other interpretations from the outside world. Venture capitalist Marc Andreessen questioned whether Anthropic was truly limiting its release due to security concerns, or whether it was due to a lack of sufficient computing power to support large-scale public use. Andreessen mentioned that Anthropic has recently experienced frequent service outages and limited users' computing power supply during peak hours. Li Guanghui expressed his disagreement with Anthropic's approach: "In essence, this so-called 'defensive' approach prioritizes opening up model capabilities to American software and cloud service providers such as Apple, Google, Microsoft, and IBM. Security should be verified through open confrontation, not defined and endorsed unilaterally by a few entities. If the standards, testing environment, and results of security assessments are not transparent, then this 'security' itself lacks credibility." A point we need to pay close attention to is that both Anthropic's Mythos and OpenAI's GPT-5.4-Cyber ​​are security tools that prioritize serving the American technology ecosystem. If a key technology is only open to specific countries or a few institutions, rather than spreading globally across industries, it often gradually develops into a "technological barrier" or even "technological dominance." Similar situations have already been observed in other fields, such as the US's long-term export restrictions on NVIDIA's high-end GPUs (such as H200 and B300) to certain countries, and early controls on encryption technologies. Once core capabilities are restricted from flowing, the industry structure often changes accordingly. This deviates somewhat from a long-standing practice in the cybersecurity industry. Over the past few decades, "ethical hacking" has gradually become a mainstream practice, coupled with responsible coordinated disclosure mechanisms, encouraging more security researchers to participate and improve overall system security by discovering vulnerabilities and disclosing problems. While not a rule strictly followed by everyone, it has always been a crucial foundation for the traditional cybersecurity industry. What we need to consider is: in such a closed alliance, will this power of definition begin to concentrate in the hands of a few countries or entities? If so, the methods of cybersecurity assessment, verification paths, and even the power of discourse may undergo significant changes. These changes ultimately return to a more practical question: how will each participant in the industry reposition itself? In short, traditional security vendors need to figure out their future path as soon as possible.

data.title: One Vote Keeps Trump’s Iran War Alive—House Showdown Exposes Cracks in GOP Ranks
data.description:
A single vote was all that stood between de-escalation and continuation. In a razor-thin 213–214 decision, the House rejected a war powers resolution that would have forced President Donald Trump to halt US military involvement in Iran without explicit congressional approval—handing the administration a critical victory while exposing fractures inside Republican ranks.

BREAKING:

🇺🇸🇮🇷 The House narrowly failed to pass a resolution to end Trump’s war with Iran, losing by a single vote, 213–214.

Only one Democrat, Jared Golden, voted with Republicans to allow the war effort to continue. pic.twitter.com/jHVefaAeS9

— Megatron (@Megatron_ron) April 16, 2026

The measure, introduced by Gregory Meeks, accused the president of dragging the United States into an unauthorized conflict. But despite unified Democratic support, the resolution collapsed under near-total Republican opposition—save for one notable break.

Thomas Massie stood alone as the only Republican to back the resolution, maintaining his long-held stance on limiting executive war powers. On the other side, Jared Golden was the sole Democrat to vote against it, while Warren Davidson voted “present” and three Republicans failed to vote altogether—absences that ultimately tightened the margin and sealed the resolution’s fate.

The vote mirrors a similar rejection in the Senate just a day earlier, reinforcing a clear pattern: Congress remains unwilling—or unable—to rein in the president’s authority over the Iran conflict.

President Trump on Truth Social: So, there you have it, the meeting went well, most points were agreed to, but the only point that really mattered, NUCLEAR, was not.

Effective immediately, the United States Navy, the Finest in the World, will begin the process of BLOCKADING any… pic.twitter.com/OtgoJyD8AI

— Donald J Trump Posts TruthSocial (@TruthTrumpPost) April 12, 2026

But this was never just about passing legislation. Democrats have repeatedly forced these votes to put Republicans on record defending a war that is becoming increasingly unpopular at home.

With oil markets rattled by tensions around the Strait of Hormuz, rising fuel and fertilizer costs are feeding economic anxiety in key districts—turning foreign policy into a domestic political liability ahead of the 2026 midterms.

Jeff Currie of Carlyle went on live television and said the oil market is completely mispriced.

He said futures price crude at around $100 a barrel, but physical oil delivered to Asian refiners is actually costing between $130 and $170.

At one point this month, Oman crude, the… https://t.co/s23MtBWRxXpic.twitter.com/84lckjm8Hj

— StockMarket.News (@_Investinq) April 2, 2026

At its core, the standoff revives a long-running constitutional debate. While Congress holds the power to declare war, successive administrations have expanded the scope of unilateral military action. Lawmakers have leaned on the War Powers Resolution of 1973 to challenge that authority, but each attempt has fallen short, with Republicans consistently siding with the executive branch.

For markets, the implications are immediate. The Iran conflict has become one of the defining macro risks of 2026, with oil, equities, and even cryptocurrencies reacting sharply to any signal of escalation or de-escalation. The failure of this resolution removes a key legislative off-ramp, leaving diplomacy—rather than Congress—as the only viable path to easing tensions.

In the end, the outcome underscores a stark reality: the war continues not with overwhelming support, but by the narrowest of margins. One vote was enough to keep it alive—and that margin may now define both US foreign policy and the political battle heading into November.

data.title: Six charts to understand Coingecko's Q1 crypto industry report
data.description:
Source: Coingecko; Compiled by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

In the first quarter of 2026, with the downward trend at the end of 2025 compounded by global geopolitical turmoil, the cryptocurrency market transitioned from a significant correction to a sustained "crypto winter." The total market capitalization of the crypto market fell by 20.4%, wiping out $622 billion, closing at $2.4 trillion at the end of the first quarter of 2026, a drop of approximately 45% from the high point in October 2025. In mid-January, Kevin Warsh was nominated as Chairman of the Federal Reserve, signaling a possible shift in US monetary policy towards a hawkish stance, further accelerating the market contraction.

Despite the overall market weakness, this quarter saw a clear flow of safe-haven funds, and the dominant positions of exchanges and public blockchains underwent significant changes. This "Crypto Industry Report Q1 2026" covers the overall crypto market landscape, Bitcoin and Ethereum analysis, in-depth analysis of the decentralized finance (DeFi) ecosystem, and a performance review of centralized exchanges (CEXs) and decentralized exchanges (DEXs).   1. The total market capitalization of cryptocurrencies fell by 20.4% in Q1 2026, closing at $2.4 trillion at the end of March.

  2. The total market capitalization of stablecoins remained flat at $309.9 billion, with USDT supply experiencing its first decline since Q2 2022.

  3. Crude oil led the market in Q1 2026, with a gain of 76.9%; Bitcoin fell 22.0% in tandem with the stock market.

  4. Centralized exchange spot trading volume fell by 39.1% to $2.7 trillion in Q1 2026, after hitting a high of $0.8 trillion in March.
